constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains three space‑separated integers N, A and B — the number of walls, the number of painters and the time required to paint one unit height of a wall. The second line contains N space‑separated integers C1, C2, …, CN, where Ci is the height of the i‑th wall.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum total time required to paint all walls.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One painter will paint first wall, other painter will paint second one. So first painter will use 202020 units time, other painter will use 252525 units time. So answer will be maximum of them which will be 252525.
Test case 222: One painter will paint walls [2,8,2][2, 8, 2][2,8,2], other will paint walls [6,6][6, 6][6,6]. So first painter will use 120120120 units time, other painter will use 120120120 units time. So answer will be maximum of them which will be 120120120.

solution.approach: The problem is equivalent to partitioning the array of wall heights into at most A contiguous groups so that the maximum sum of heights in any group is as small as possible. If the maximum group sum is X, each painter needs X·B time, and the total time equals the maximum painter time. We binary‑search the answer X between max(Ci) and sum(Ci). For a candidate X we greedily scan the walls, accumulating heights until adding the next wall would exceed X, then we start a new painter. The number of painters needed is counted; if it is <= A the candidate is feasible. The smallest feasible X multiplied by B is the answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log S) per test case, where S = sum of all Ci. The total over all test cases is O((total N)·log S).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra besides the input array (or O(N) if the heights are stored).
